items.title: Fuel efficiency vs engine displacement
items.scenario: An automotive analyst plots miles-per-gallon against engine displacement. The scatter bends downward: small engines gain efficiency quickly, but the drop levels off for large engines. A straight line misses the flattening tail; a degree-2 polynomial captures the curvature with one extra feature (displacement squared).
items.explanation: Adding x^2 lets the fitted curve bend without abandoning the interpretable regression framework.

items.title: Weekly sales growth tapering over time
items.scenario: A startup tracks weekly revenue after launch. Growth is steep early, then slows as the market saturates. Fitting week number with a degree-3 polynomial gives an S-shaped trend that a single linear slope cannot represent.
items.explanation: Time-series curvature is a common one-dimensional polynomial use case when you need a smooth trend, not a step function.

items.title: Sensor calibration curve
items.scenario: A temperature sensor's raw ADC reading is linear near room temperature but compresses at extremes. Calibration maps reading -> Celsius with a degree-2 or degree-3 polynomial fit on labeled calibration points, stored as a few coefficients on the device.
items.explanation: Low-degree polynomials are compact, fast to evaluate at inference, and sufficient for smooth hardware non-linearities.
